facebook.content_angle: The bilateral vs unilateral breathing debate — when breathing to your strong side is the right race call, and the drill that builds true bilateral control so you always have the choice
facebook.post_text:
Your breathing pattern is making that decision for you — and it's costing you. 🌊

Most swimmers default to one side without thinking. That works until it doesn't — crosswind in open water, a competitor pushing on your blind side, or a finish where your head turns the wrong way.

👉 Bilateral breathing (every 3 strokes) builds balanced body rotation and reduces drag
👉 Unilateral to your strong side delivers more oxygen in the final 50m when it matters most
👉 The problem isn't which one you choose — it's not having both options available

The swimmers who win races don't just breathe — they breathe strategically. 🔥

✔️ Train bilaterally in every session to build the habit and symmetry
✔️ In race simulation sets, switch to your strong side to rehearse the real scenario
✔️ Drill: 300m alternating every 50 between bilateral and unilateral — your body will tell you where the weakness is

Are you training your breathing pattern, or just hoping it holds up on race day?
facebook.engagement_bait: Drop your default breathing pattern below — every 2, every 3, or somewhere in between.
